title: Why Your Sourdough Is Flat: A Practical Walkthrough
author: Channel Persona
text:
Okay so I want to do something a little different today. I get a lot of messages from people whose sourdough is coming out flat and dense and they do not know why. And the thing is, the reasons are almost always one of about five issues. I am going to walk through all five, with examples, and by the end you should be able to diagnose your own loaf.

The first issue, and this is the one people underestimate the most, is starter strength. Your starter is the engine of the entire bread. If the engine is weak, nothing downstream matters. You can have the most beautiful flour, perfect hydration, perfect shaping, beautiful scoring, the best oven in the world, and if your starter was not ready when you mixed the dough, you are going to get a flat loaf. Every time.

How do you know if your starter is ready. Three tests. First test, the float test. You drop a small spoonful of active starter in a glass of water. If it floats, there is enough gas in it that it can lift dough. If it sinks, it is not ready. This test is not perfect but it is quick and it is useful.

Second test, the volume test. You mark the level of your starter on the jar right after you feed it. You watch it. A healthy starter should roughly double in volume within four to six hours at room temperature. If it takes twelve hours to double, your starter is sluggish and you need to feed it more often and keep it warmer. If it is not doubling at all, you have a fundamental problem and we need to talk about that separately.

Third test, the smell. A healthy active starter smells yeasty and a little bit sweet, with maybe a hint of sour. A starter that smells like nail polish remover, which is acetone, is hungry. Feed it and wait. A starter that smells like vomit is overproofed and the acids have taken over. Pour off most of it, feed what is left with a generous ratio, like one to five to five, and give it two feeding cycles to recover.

Okay, second issue. Underfermentation. This is by far the most common cause of flat sourdough in the loaves I see. People are underfermenting their bulk because they are going by clock time rather than by dough signs. Your recipe says bulk for five hours. That is just a guideline. The actual bulk time depends on your starter, your flour, the temperature of your kitchen, and how much starter you used. On a cold day in January, five hours might be way too short. On a hot day in July, five hours might be too long.

So what do you look at instead of the clock. You look at the dough. You look at volume increase. A well-bulked dough should have gained between fifty and seventy-five percent in volume. Not doubled. Just gained. If you doubled it, you are probably overfermented. If you gained twenty percent, you are underfermented. You look at the surface. A well-bulked dough looks domed and smooth on top with a few bubbles just under the surface. You look at the sides if you are using a straight-sided container. You should see big irregular bubbles pressed against the glass. If the sides look like still water, you are underfermented.

The fix for underfermentation is patience and warmth. Most home kitchens are colder than bakeries. A bakery runs at seventy-eight, eighty degrees Fahrenheit. Your kitchen is probably sixty-seven. That single difference can easily double your bulk time. Put your dough in the oven with just the light on, or in the microwave with a cup of hot water next to it, or on top of the fridge. Find the warm spot in your kitchen and use it.

Third issue. Overfermentation. This is the opposite problem. Your dough sat too long, the gluten network broke down, and now it cannot hold gas any more. The bread bakes flat not because it never rose, but because it rose and then collapsed before you got it in the oven. You can usually tell if this is what happened by feeling the dough. Overfermented dough feels slack and wet and it will not hold shape. It flows rather than stretches. When you try to shape it, it fights you by refusing to grip itself. You end up with a puddle.

The fix for overfermentation is harder than the fix for underfermentation, because you cannot un-overferment a dough. Once the gluten is gone, it is gone. What you can do next time is shorten your bulk. Shorter bulk, shorter cold retard, less starter in the recipe, cooler fermentation temperature. Any of those will help. The single most useful intervention is to weigh your starter. People's eyes lie about what fifty grams of starter looks like. A kitchen scale does not.

Fourth issue. Shaping. This is the one I see most often in loaves that look almost right but are just a little too flat. The fermentation was fine. The dough was handled okay during bulk. But the shaping did not build enough surface tension, so when the loaf went into the oven, it spread before it had a chance to spring.

Shaping is a motor skill, and I cannot teach it to you in a video. What I can do is describe what you are trying to do. You are trying to create a taut drum skin of gluten on the outside of the loaf. That skin is what holds the shape in the oven while the interior springs up. If the skin is loose, the loaf spreads. If the skin is tight, the loaf holds.

The two common shaping mistakes I see are, one, people are too gentle. They have heard you should treat sourdough with love, and they interpret that as barely touching it. That is wrong. You should treat it with confidence. You fold it, you roll it, you press it against the counter. You build that skin. Two, people do not rest the dough enough between preshape and final shape. Preshape, then rest for fifteen to thirty minutes covered, then final shape. If you skip the rest, the gluten is too tight to work and you will tear it.

Okay, fifth issue. Oven setup. This is the one people fix last because it requires buying something or setting something up. If the other four things are perfect and your loaf is still flat, it is probably your oven. Specifically, it is probably your lack of steam.

When a loaf hits the oven, there is a window of about ten or fifteen minutes where the surface is still soft enough to expand. This is the oven spring window. During this window, the yeast is still alive and producing gas at a huge rate because the dough is warming. If the surface is moist during this window, it stays pliable and the loaf springs. If the surface is dry during this window, it sets into a crust immediately, and the loaf cannot spring even though the gas is there. It is trapped under a hard shell.

The fix is steam. A cast iron dutch oven is the easiest solution because the dough steams itself inside the closed pot. Preheat it for an hour with your oven, slide the dough in, put the lid on, and the steam just happens. This is why dutch ovens are so popular for sourdough. They are almost idiot-proof for the oven-spring problem.

If you do not have a dutch oven, you can add steam manually. Preheat a metal pan on the bottom rack. When you put your loaf in, pour about a cup of boiling water into the preheated pan. It will flash to steam. Close the oven fast. The steam will keep the surface of your loaf pliable for the first ten or fifteen minutes. Then you can open the oven, remove the pan, and let the loaf finish in the dry heat, which is what crisps the crust.

Those are the five big issues. Starter strength, underfermentation, overfermentation, shaping, oven setup. Let us go through a diagnostic flow so you can figure out which one is causing your problem.

If your crumb is dense and gummy throughout, with tight small holes, almost no open structure, and the loaf is a low dome, it is almost always either weak starter or underfermentation. Check your starter first. Do the three tests. If your starter passes, lengthen your bulk next time.

If your crumb is somewhat open in the middle but the loaf is flat and spread out, with a pale flabby crust, it is almost always overfermentation. Shorten your bulk, use less starter, cooler temperature.

If your crumb is open and the loaf has some height but looks like a flying saucer, wide and low, it is shaping. Work on your shaping. Watch videos of people shaping dough. Practice on cheap flour batches just to build the motor skill.

If your crumb is open and tall but the crust is pale and the top did not bloom along the scoring line, it is your oven. Steam. More steam, sooner, hotter.

If your crumb has giant holes at the top and dense compacted dough at the bottom, this is a specific failure mode. It means you bench-rested with the seam down when it should have been up, or vice versa. It means the gas floated to the top without being redistributed. It is fixable by shaping the loaf in a different orientation or by doing a more aggressive preshape to redistribute the gas.

A few more tips that are not quite big enough to be on the main list but that I want to mention. Hydration. The higher your hydration, the more skill you need to handle the dough. People see seventy-eight percent hydration loaves on Instagram and try to replicate them on their second week of baking. That is a mistake. Start at sixty-five percent. Get that working. Then go to seventy. Then seventy-five. Add five percent at a time. Your dough will be easier to shape and easier to read at lower hydration, and the bread will still be great.

Flour. Not all bread flour is equal. A strong bread flour with thirteen or fourteen percent protein will make a much better loaf than a weaker all-purpose flour at twelve percent. If your bread is consistently flat and you have ruled out everything else, try upgrading your flour. King Arthur bread flour is a reliable baseline in the United States. In Europe, look for T65 or type 550 flour with protein in that range. Freshly milled flour is wonderful but it ferments very differently from commercial flour, so only take that leap if you are ready to relearn some things.

Water. Most tap water is fine for baking. If you live in a city with very heavily chlorinated water, you might want to let the water sit out overnight to let the chlorine dissipate. I have not seen this matter much in practice. If you are using well water with a lot of mineral content, it usually helps fermentation rather than hurting it. Softened water, the stuff from a water softener that uses salt, can actually slow fermentation. Avoid softened water for bread.

Salt. Two percent of the flour weight. This is almost the only ratio in sourdough I will state as a rule. Less than one and a half percent and your dough ferments too fast and the bread is bland. More than two and a half percent and your dough ferments too slowly and it tastes salty. Two percent is the target. Measure it on a scale.

Temperature. I mentioned this but it deserves its own paragraph. The single variable that new bakers most underestimate is temperature. It matters at every stage. It matters for your starter, for your bulk, for your final proof, for your oven. A thermometer is the cheapest upgrade you can make to your setup. A digital probe thermometer costs fifteen dollars. It will teach you more about why your bread is doing what it is doing than any book or video.

Retarding. A cold overnight retard in the refrigerator has two purposes. It develops flavor, because the slow cold fermentation produces different acids than a warm fast fermentation. It also makes the dough easier to score, because it is firmer. A cold retard is not strictly necessary. You can make excellent bread without it. But most of the breads that look beautiful on Instagram have been cold retarded overnight, and the ear, the dramatic ridge of crust that pops up from the scoring, is much easier to get from a cold-retarded loaf than from a warm one.

Scoring. Confident, single-stroke cuts. Not hesitant sawing motions. The blade should be sharp enough and held at the right angle, which is not straight down but slightly slanted, so that you create a flap of dough rather than a straight split. That flap is what lifts to form the ear. If your blade is dull, your dough is dragging along the edge and tearing rather than slicing cleanly.

Okay, we have covered a lot. Let me zoom back out. The honest truth about sourdough is that it takes about twenty loaves before it starts to feel natural. The first ten loaves are diagnostic. You are learning what your starter does, what your kitchen temperature does, what your flour is doing. The next ten loaves are calibration. You are dialing in your timing and your shaping. By loaf twenty, most people are consistently producing good bread. By loaf fifty, they are producing excellent bread and teaching other people.

Do not get discouraged at loaf three. Everybody has a stack of flat sourdoughs behind them. Those are the tuition you pay to get to the bread you actually want to be making. Keep a log. Write down your start time, your starter weight, your flour, your hydration, your bulk time, your bulk temperature, your final proof, your shape, your oven temperature, and a photo of the loaf. After ten loaves, you will be able to see your own patterns, and you will fix your bread yourself, without needing a video like this one.

That is the whole thing. Before I go, if you found this useful, the way you help me out is by subscribing. Next week I want to do a deeper dive on starter maintenance because that is where most people are quietly losing, even when they do not realize it. Thanks for watching.
